metadata.summary: A selection matrix for choosing one of nine "minimum product" frameworks — MVP, MLP, MMP, MAC, RAT, MDP, MVA, MFP, SLC — before the first spec.md is written.

content.title: Minimum Product Frameworks
content.summary: A selection matrix for choosing one of nine "minimum product" frameworks — MVP, MLP, MMP, MAC, RAT, MDP, MVA, MFP, SLC — before the first spec.md is written. The matrix maps market condition (blue/red ocean), buyer type (B2B/consumer), differentiator, and technical uncertainty to the right framework. Each choice must be versioned in a framework-choice.md with two explicit exit criteria before scoping begins.

why.p: "Just build an MVP" is the wrong default in crowded markets where users already have "good enough" alternatives. In red oceans, MVP can be strategically suicidal: users will not switch without a lovable, marketable, or technically superior alternative. The matrix forces a defensible framework choice based on market evidence, not reflex.

list.item: New product or major module — before the first spec.md exists.
Team is reflexively saying "let's ship an MVP" without checking market density, buyer type, or differentiator.
Pivot moment: current build is failing on retention or conversion — re-pick the framework before re-scoping.
Multiple stakeholders disagree on what "minimum" means — use the matrix as a forcing function.
Pre-investment or pre-board memo: justify the chosen framework against blue/red ocean and ICP positioning.
Methodology already chosen and validated — go straight to that framework's scoping doc; do not re-litigate.
Pure feature work inside a shipped product — use release-planning, feature-prioritization-rice, or MoSCoW instead.
Hard-deadline regulated launches where scope is dictated by compliance, not strategy.
Tiny fix-it-fast tasks (<1 sprint) — framework choice overhead exceeds value.
Internal tools with one stakeholder — pick MFP and move on.
MVP (Minimum Viable Product): test core assumptions in a new market with an unvalidated idea.
MLP (Minimum Lovable Product): create emotional connection in a crowded market requiring differentiation.
MMP (Minimum Marketable Product): generate revenue in B2B or enterprise sales contexts where a sellable surface is required.
MAC (Minimum Awesome Component): differentiate via one killer feature where feature-based differentiation is the strategic bet.
RAT (Riskiest Assumption Test): validate the biggest risk in a high-uncertainty project before any full build.
MDP (Minimum Delightful Product): exceed expectations in a premium positioning context where delight is the brand promise.
MVA (Minimum Valuable Product): deliver measurable business value in enterprise or ROI-focused contexts.
MFP (Minimum Functional Product): prove technical feasibility first in complex technology or infrastructure projects.
SLC (Simple, Lovable, Complete): balance simplicity and polish in consumer apps where all three attributes are simultaneously required.

rule.statement: Apply the decision matrix: blue ocean unvalidated → MVP or RAT; red ocean many competitors → MLP or MAC; enterprise B2B → MMP or MVA; consumer emotion-driven → MLP or MDP; technical uncertainty → MFP then expand.
rule.rationale: The default "MVP" is correct only in genuinely uncontested markets. In red oceans, users already have "good enough" alternatives — an MVP that is merely functional gives them no reason to switch.

rule.statement: Every framework choice must include two explicit exit criteria — one quantitative (metric + threshold + measurement window) and one disqualifying kill criterion — before scoping begins. Without exit criteria the framework is decorative.
rule.rationale: Teams pick "MLP" because it sounds nicer than "MVP" without changing scope. The exit criteria are the only mechanism that enforces the actual definition of "done enough to advance."

rule.statement: Pick exactly one framework per scope cycle. "MVP+MLP hybrid" is a hedge that produces neither validation rigor nor delight investment. Force the choice, then re-pick on the next pivot.
rule.rationale: Hybrid choices distribute effort across two conflicting optimization targets — speed-of-learning (MVP) and quality-of-experience (MLP) — and optimize for neither.

rule.statement: When 3+ "good enough" competitors exist, the default framework flips to MLP or SLC. In crowded markets, "viable" is table stakes — users will not switch for a product that merely works.
rule.rationale: Users in crowded markets bear switching costs (data migration, habit change, team retraining). They will absorb those costs only for a product that is clearly better on an emotional or economic dimension, not merely functional.

antipattern.description: Reflexively choosing MVP without checking competitor density, buyer type, or differentiator.
antipattern.reason: "Just build an MVP" is a 2010s default that does not generalize to 2026 categories where AI-assisted competitors can clone functional surfaces in weeks. Framework choice must be grounded in market evidence.

rule.statement: Pass 1 — Research: a market-researcher agent gathers competitor count, ICP, buyer type, switching cost, and risk profile into market-context.yml before the selection agent runs. Refuse to run the matrix if market-context.yml is missing or buyer is "unknown."
rule.rationale: Agents lacking market context will default to MVP (training bias from 2010s startup canon). Requiring a completed market-context.yml as a pre-condition forces evidence-first selection.

rule.statement: Pass 2 — Selection: a PM agent applies the decision matrix to market-context.yml, scores all nine frameworks, and emits framework-choice.md with the chosen framework, the rejected ones, and two exit criteria. Score every framework before naming the winner.
rule.rationale: Scoring rejected frameworks forces honest disagreement rationale and prevents post-hoc justification of a predetermined choice.

rule.statement: Pass 3 — Hand-off: the chosen framework's downstream methodology agent (mvp-scoping, mlp-planning, etc.) consumes framework-choice.md as its input contract. Abort and request re-pick if the choice file is older than 14 days or refers to a different framework.
rule.rationale: Stale choice files decouple framework and scope; a sprint that starts with an outdated framework choice ships the wrong minimum product.

rule.statement: Re-run the matrix on every pivot — failed validation, missed activation target, or strategy shift. Append the new decision as a dated section in framework-choice.md; never rewrite history.
rule.rationale: The pivot history is the most useful single input to next year's selection heuristics. Rewriting history destroys institutional memory about what conditions trigger which framework failures.

rule.statement: MFP is a stepping stone, not a destination. Pre-commit the trigger that ends MFP (e.g., "first internal demo passes", "API latency < 100ms at 1k RPS") before MFP work starts.
rule.rationale: Without an explicit graduation trigger, teams stay in MFP indefinitely because "technical feasibility" is never fully resolved — there is always one more edge case.

antipattern.description: Acronym soup as theatre: teams pick "MLP" because it sounds nicer than "MVP" without changing scope or adding exit criteria.
antipattern.reason: The framework name is irrelevant without a written exit criterion per framework. The choice is decorative unless it changes what gets built and when you stop.

antipattern.description: Single-axis market read: using only blue/red ocean without considering buyer (B2B/consumer), distribution (PLG/sales-led), or regulatory load.
antipattern.reason: The nine-framework table under-specifies; in practice buyer type and distribution model often dominate the framework choice more than competitive density alone.

antipattern.description: No transition rule: README says "MFP then expand" but doesn't define what triggers the transition (metric? time? validation event?).
antipattern.reason: Agents will sit in MFP forever without an explicit graduation trigger and acceptance criteria.

antipattern.description: MAC ambiguity: "Minimum Awesome Component" overlaps heavily with MLP and SLC; in red oceans a MAC often is the lovable hook, double-counting the same scope.
antipattern.reason: The methodology doesn't fully disambiguate MAC from MLP; agents may conflate them without additional context about the category.

antipattern.description: MVA vs MMP overlap: Both target enterprise/ROI; the methodology doesn't disambiguate.
antipattern.reason: In practice, MMP = sellable, MVA = measurable business value — agents conflate them without clear distinction.

antipattern.description: No cost dimension: all nine frameworks treated as equally expensive in the decision matrix.
antipattern.reason: MLP and MDP cost 2-4x MVP for the polish budget; recommending MLP without surfacing the cost delta produces naive choices that surprise engineering in sprint planning.

antipattern.description: Framework lock-in: Once "MLP" is in the deck, switching to RAT after a failed validation is socially expensive.
antipattern.reason: Methodology offers no de-escalation path. Re-picks should be reframed as learning, not failure.

antipattern.description: Survivorship-bias source: "MVP is suicide in 2026" is a strong claim drawn from crowded-SaaS storytelling; doesn't generalise to vertical SaaS, B2B niches, or dev-tools where MVPs still win.
antipattern.reason: The framework is weighted toward horizontal SaaS assumptions; vertical and deep-tech contexts may validate differently.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Treat the matrix as a routing decision, not a build instruction. Drive it with a 3-pass subagent flow: (1) a research agent gathers market-condition evidence (competitor count, alternatives, ICP, buyer, switching cost) — emits a structured market-context.yml; (2) a PM agent applies the decision matrix to that file and emits a framework-choice.md with the chosen framework, the rejected ones, and the exit criterion; (3) the chosen framework's downstream methodology agent (e.g. mvp-scoping, mlp-planning) consumes that file as its input contract. Re-pick on every pivot (failed validation, missed activation target). The choice file is versioned in .aidocs/product_docs/framework-choice.md — diffing it across pivots is the audit trail.

list.item: faion-product-manager: orchestrator. Reads the matrix, calls research agent, emits the choice file.
faion-market-researcher-agent: produces market-context inputs: competitor density, ICP, buyer type, switching cost. Required before the matrix can be applied.
faion-research-agent modes validate, personas, pains: confirms the chosen framework's assumption (e.g. "users will pay for lovable" or "buyer cares about ROI").
mvp-scoping / mlp-planning / sibling methodology agents: downstream consumers of the choice; one fires depending on the matrix output.
faion-sdd-executor-agent: converts the chosen framework's scope into a spec.md → design.md → tasks chain.
brainstorm skill: diverge/converge when the matrix is genuinely tied between two frameworks (e.g. MLP vs MDP); avoids single-LLM lock-in.
framework-pivot-detector agent (worth creating): re-runs the matrix when an activation/retention/win-rate metric crosses a threshold; emits a "re-pick recommended" alert with rationale.

section.name: prompt-patterns
section.code:
You are a product manager applying the 9 minimum product frameworks matrix. Inputs: <market-context.yml> and <icp.yml>.
For each of MVP, MLP, MMP, MAC, RAT, MDP, MVA, MFP, SLC: - score 0-3 on fit with market_condition + buyer + differentiator - list the single strongest reason for and against Pick the top-1 with a 1-sentence rationale and write 2 explicit exit criteria (metric + threshold). Output framework-choice.md. Refuse to pick if any of: market_condition is missing, buyer is "unknown", or two frameworks tie above score 2 — return "need-discovery" and list the gap.

You are a PM auditor. Inputs: framework-choice.md (current), last-30-days metrics snapshot, post-mortem of the failed validation.
Decide: stay (state why), pivot to <framework> (state why and what changes), or escalate (matrix can't decide — name the missing data). No prose unless one of the three. Append decision to framework-choice.md as a new dated section; never rewrite history.

You are <chosen-framework>-scoping agent (e.g. mlp-planning). Input: framework-choice.md. Constraint: do not re-litigate the choice. Produce: scope.md with in/out lists, the one assumption this framework is built to test, the kill-criterion. If the input file is older than 14 days or refers to a different framework, abort and request a re-pick.

list.item: gh CLI: PR-review the framework-choice.md so PM + founder co-sign. https://cli.github.com
claude (Anthropic CLI): Run the 3-pass selection flow headlessly via cron / makefile target. https://docs.anthropic.com
dbt / metabase: Encode framework exit criteria as SQL models so "did we hit MVP exit?" is auditable. https://docs.getdbt.com
growthbook / statsig: Encode the RAT (riskiest assumption test) as a feature flag + experiment with a stop condition. https://docs.growthbook.io
posthog (HogQL): Pull activation / retention / NPS for MLP and MDP exit-criterion verification. https://posthog.com/docs
typeform / tally API: Cheap qualitative validation for MLP "lovable" claim before any code. https://www.typeform.com/developers
productboard API: If used, sync the chosen framework + exit criteria to roadmap items. https://developer.productboard.com
linear / jira API: Tag every initiative with the framework label; agents use it to filter. https://developers.linear.app
notion API: Versioned framework-choice doc with comment-thread history. https://developers.notion.com
pmf-survey (Sean Ellis) tooling: "How would you feel if you couldn't use this product?" — exit-criterion proxy for MVP→MLP transition. https://pmfsurvey.com
Productboard (SaaS, API yes): Tag features by framework; agents filter MVP-vs-MLP scope.
Aha! (SaaS, API yes): Roadmap layer; encode framework as a custom field.
Linear / Jira (SaaS, API yes): Initiative-level framework tag; sprint planning respects it.
Notion / Confluence (SaaS, API yes): framework-choice.md lives here when not in repo.
Mixpanel / Amplitude / PostHog (SaaS / OSS, API yes): Exit-criterion measurement; agents pull cohorts for re-pick decisions.
Hotjar / FullStory / LogRocket (SaaS, API limited): Qualitative signal for MLP/MDP "lovable / delightful" claims.
Sprig / Survicate / Maze (SaaS, API yes): In-product surveys; cheap MLP validation without code changes.
LaunchDarkly / GrowthBook / Statsig (SaaS / OSS, API yes): Encode RAT as a flag with a stop rule; agents control rollout.
Userpilot / Appcues / Pendo (SaaS, API yes): MDP onboarding polish layer without engineering churn.
ProductPlan (SaaS, API yes): Roadmap visualisation per framework type.
Gainsight (SaaS, API yes): MMP / MVA buyers — track ROI-style adoption metrics.
Reforge / Lenny's library (content, n/a): Framework selection essays; LLM context for prompt grounding.
Pair every framework choice with two exit criteria — one quantitative (metric + threshold) and one disqualifying (kill-criterion). Without exit criteria the framework is theatre.
Version the choice file in git, not Notion. framework-choice.md belongs next to the spec; pivots show as commits.
Default to MVP only when market is genuinely uncontested. If 3+ "good enough" competitors exist, the default flips to MLP or SLC; "we'll iterate to lovable later" rarely happens.
Pick exactly one framework per scope cycle. "MVP+MLP hybrid" is a hedge; force a single choice and let the next cycle re-pick.
MFP is a stepping stone, not a destination. Pre-commit to the trigger (e.g. "first internal demo passes") that ends MFP and enters MVP/MLP.
MMP / MVA need a sales letter before code. Write the buyer's purchase-justification memo first; if it doesn't read as compelling, the framework is wrong.
Run a "framework retro" 30 days post-launch. Did the chosen framework's exit criteria fire? Was the rejected alternative actually better in hindsight? Feed back into selection heuristics.
Keep MAC use rare. A killer feature without a product is a demo, not a business; require explicit founder sign-off before picking MAC.
Score every framework, not just the winner. Forces honest disagreement rationale; hides nothing in the prompt log.
In red oceans, lead with the differentiator type, not the framework. "Lovable, marketable, or delightful" is the real choice; MLP/MMP/MDP are wrappers.

antipattern.description: Acronym hallucination: LLMs invent extra frameworks (MUP, MEP, MWP) under prompt pressure.
antipattern.reason: Constrain to the exact 9-row enum; reject any output naming a framework outside the list.

antipattern.description: Default-to-MVP drift: Any LLM trained on 2010s startup canon will pick MVP unless explicitly told to consider crowded-market evidence.
antipattern.reason: Force the agent to print the matrix score per framework before naming the winner.

antipattern.description: Crowded-market over-claim: Without competitor evidence, agents will declare every market "crowded" to justify MLP.
antipattern.reason: Require competitor_count + named competitors in market-context.yml; refuse to pick if missing.

antipattern.description: Exit-criterion vagueness: Agents emit "good user feedback" or "high adoption" as exit criteria.
antipattern.reason: Validate that each criterion has a metric, a threshold, and a measurement window.

antipattern.description: Framework-then-justify failure: Agent picks first, rationalises second.
antipattern.reason: Mitigate by structured output: scores per framework BEFORE the chosen one, in a JSON schema the prompt enforces.

antipattern.description: Confounding with mvp-scoping methodology: Agent jumps straight into in/out scope before picking the framework.
antipattern.reason: Pipeline must enforce: choice file exists → only then scoping methodology runs.

antipattern.description: Pivot inertia: Agent re-confirms the original framework even when metrics scream pivot.
antipattern.reason: Force a structured "stay vs pivot" decision with rejection-reason for the rejected option.

antipattern.description: No human checkpoint on enterprise commitments: MMP/MVA implies sales contracts and SLAs — agents must not autonomously promote to these without a founder/PM sign-off step.
antipattern.reason: Add a human gate before MMP/MVA selection goes live.

antipattern.description: Hand-off contract drift: Downstream methodology agents read the choice file; if framework changes mid-cycle without re-running the downstream agent, scope and framework drift apart.
antipattern.reason: CI: hash the choice file in the spec front-matter; mismatch fails the build.

antipattern.description: 2026-claim staleness: README says "in 2026 MVP is suicide in crowded markets" — agents will quote this verbatim in pitches.
antipattern.reason: Phrase it as a heuristic, not a law, to avoid over-confident decks.

section.name: pick-framework-script
section.p: The README ships only the matrix table. The missing artefact is a machine-readable framework-choice file the downstream agents can consume. The pick-framework.sh script is intentionally rule-based (not LLM) so the choice is auditable; the LLM agent fills the exit-criteria stubs and reviews the rationale.

section.name: hand-off-contracts
section.p: Framework choice ↔ downstream. Artifacts, owners, readers, formats, and cadence:

list.item: market-context.yml: owned by market-researcher agent, read by PM agent, YAML in .aidocs/product_docs/, per scope cycle.
framework-choice.md: owned by PM agent, read by mvp-scoping / mlp-planning / RAT agents, markdown dated history, per scope cycle, append-only.
Exit-criteria block (inside choice file): owned by PM, read by analytics agent, YAML inside markdown, reviewed weekly.
Re-pick decision: owned by framework-pivot-detector, read by PM and founder, new section in choice file, triggered by metric breach.
Framework retro: owned by PM, read by team, .aidocs/product_docs/retros/<date>.md, 30 days post-launch.

template.purpose: Versioned choice document skeleton: chosen framework, rationale, exit criteria, history section.
Bash + Python: rule-based first-pass framework selection from market-context.yml; emits choice file for LLM review.

script.purpose: Applies decision rules to market-context.yml; writes framework-choice.md; commits it to git.
